results.possibleIdentity: William Soghor is an artist and industrial fabricator who was active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is closely associated with Milgo Industrial Inc. (now Milgo/Bufkin), a Brooklyn-based company renowned for fabricating large-scale sculptures for prominent artists such as Richard Serra, Robert Rauschenberg, and Donald Judd. Soghor's artistic career includes participation in the landmark 1973 exhibition 'Soft as Art' at the New York Cultural Center, where he was exhibited alongside major figures like Claes Oldenburg, Robert Mapplethorpe, and Hannah Wilke. His professional background also includes founding 'The William Soghor Company,' which produced specialized archival materials for the art community, specifically artwork inventory file cards designed to hold 35mm slides. His association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) likely stems from Milgo Industrial's role in providing technical and industrial fabrication services for collaborative projects between artists and engineers. Soghor is married to the portrait painter Phyllis Herfield and resides in Manhattan.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born 1931) is a Hungarian-born American conceptual artist, widely recognized as a pioneer of ecological, land, and environmental art. She became active in the late 1960s and rose to international prominence in the early 1970s. Her work is characterized by a "visual philosophy" that integrates diverse disciplines including science, mathematics, philosophy, and technology. Denes was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; archival records from 1969 show her involvement through forms, correspondence, and technical requests. Significant early works from this period include 'Rice/Tree/Burial' (1968), 'The World of Thorns' (1968), and 'The Human Argument' (1969–1970). She was also a participant in the landmark 1970 exhibition 'Software' at the Jewish Museum and was a founding member of the A.I.R. Gallery in 1972. Her practice often involves complex drawings on graph paper and large-scale site-specific interventions, such as 'Wheatfield — A Confrontation' (1982) in Lower Manhattan.

results.possibleIdentity: Paul N. Siegel (1916–2004) was a Marxist Shakespeare scholar and Professor Emeritus of English who was highly active in the late 1960s and 1970s. While not a visual artist himself, he is frequently associated with the radical art movements of that era through his seminal 1970 book, 'Leon Trotsky on Literature and Art' (Pathfinder Press). This publication, which included the 'Manifesto: Towards a Revolutionary Free Art,' provided a major theoretical and ideological foundation for artists seeking to align creative practice with social and technological change. His work is commonly cited in the bibliographies of historical studies related to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the 1960s avant-garde, particularly concerning the role of the artist in a technological society.

Nathaniel Siegel is a New York-based artist, photographer, poet, and curator. While he is a visible figure in the contemporary New York art and poetry scene—particularly through his involvement with the Poetry Project at St. Mark's Church—he appears to have been born around 1967 (making him approximately 48 in 2015). This timeline would suggest he was too young to be an established artist in the late 1960s, though his work often engages with the historical legacies of that period. He has produced art installations such as '2,117 Days of Silence' and published poetry collections like 'Tony'.

Norman Siegel is a prominent civil rights and civil liberties attorney in New York who began his legal career in 1968. Although he is a lawyer and not primarily an artist, he has a long historical association with the New York art community, often serving as a legal advocate for artists' rights and free expression. His work includes representing the sculptor Arturo Di Modica in the 'Charging Bull' vs. 'Fearless Girl' controversy and advocating for the rights of street performers. He has been a recognizable figure in public 'artistic' or performance-based activism since 1969, such as his annual public readings of the U.S. Constitution.

Neil F. Siegel is a legal scholar and attorney who wrote an influential historical analysis of the 'Resale Royalty Provisions of the Visual Artists Rights Act' (1988). He is noted for his work on the economic rights of artists, having been active in the discourse surrounding the legal protections sought by artists like Robert Rauschenberg—a co-founder of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Though not an artist himself, his scholarship is tied to the movement's efforts to reform the relationship between artists, technology, and the art market.
